title: Stocks Are Powering Through Political Risks. That Could Be Risky.
summary: U.S. stocks look to have survived another political maelstrom, with President Donald Trump backing away from his pledge to assume “total control” of Greenland in favor of a vague agreement to work with NATO allies on Arctic region security.  The president referred to this week’s market selloff, which pared around 2% from the  and briefly dragged the benchmark into negative territory for the year, as “peanuts, compared to what it’s gone up” under the first year of his second term in the White House.  Stock finished higher after his speech to the World Economic Forum on Wednesday, during which he indicated he wouldn’t use “excessive strength and force” to acquire control of the Danish-held territory, and his later remarks about a framework agreement on Greenland’s future.

title: Is it Time to Make Room for Global ETFs in Your Portfolio?
summary: Renewed transatlantic trade tensions and a complex geopolitical landscape heighten U.S. economic volatility, prompting investors to turn to global equity funds.

title: Dow tumbles more than 850 points and dollar slides over Greenland and tariff threats
summary: Investors revived the “Sell America” trade Tuesday and sold off US stocks, bonds and the dollar amid concerns about President Donald Trump’s clash with European leaders over ownership of Greenland.

title: Treasuries, Stocks Sell Off as Greenland and Japan Shatter Calm
summary: As stock markets opened on Tuesday, the “Sell America” trade came back in full force as Treasuries tumbled, driving up yields, and the dollar slid.  The moves show that investors’ previous willingness to shrug off Trump’s actions — including the White House’s capture of Venezuela’s leader, its threats to nearby countries, and renewed attacks on the Federal Reserve — is beginning to erode.  Trump’s demand for US control over Greenland is stoking investor anxieties about potential worst-case scenarios, including a rupture in the NATO alliance, a full-blown trade war, or European steps to foment market turbulence as a way to force the Trump administration to back down.

title: Volatility is Back, and It's Weighing on Bitcoin. Is Crypto a Hedge or a Risk Asset This Time?
summary: Volatility has soared after President Donald Trump's latest comments suggested the U.S. might take Greenland by force. As investors panned risk assets, the price of bitcoin also took a hit.

title: Stock market today: Dow, S&P 500 cap volatile week with back-to-back weekly losses
summary: Wall Street is faltering as it heads into the end of holiday shortened but rollercoaster week defined by Trump's moves on Greenland.

title: Gold tracks best week since 2020, silver breaches $100 in stunning rally
summary: Gold and silver continued to surge to new highs as investors flocked to precious metals amid geopolitical uncertainty and expectations for more interest rate cuts.

title: Dollar’s Worst Week Since May Comes With US ‘Policy Nightmare’
summary: The Bloomberg Dollar Spot Index tumbled 0.7% on Friday and is down 1.6% on the week, its worst performance in about eight months.  “With policy volatility rising, the US dollar has become the release valve for US risk premia,” said Erica Camilleri, senior global macro analyst at Manulife Investment Management.  Investors were whipsawed this week as US President Donald Trump first brandished tariffs on Europe over his bid for Greenland, then abruptly dropped them after striking a deal with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte at the World Economic Forum in Davos.

title: How major US stock indexes fared Friday, 1/23/2026
summary: The Dow Jones Industrial Average dipped 0.6%, and the Nasdaq composite rose 0.3%. The majority of U.S. stocks fell, including Intel, which gave a weaker financial forecast for upcoming results than analysts expected. The U.S. dollar's value slid against the Japanese yen, euro and other currencies, while gold rose to another record in a signal that nervousness remains in the market.

title: Denmark Owns $10 Billion in Treasuries. It Isn’t Completely ‘Irrelevant.’
summary: What matters isn’t how much of the debt Denmark owns. The point to focus on is the precedent raised by news that a Danish pension fund is selling all of its holdings.

title: Stocks Hold Gains Following PCE Inflation Report
summary: A key inflation reading took a little wind out of the stock market's sails, but only a little as the major indexes remained meaningfully higher in morning trading.  The core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) index, which is the Fed's preferred inflation metric, rose 0.2% in November, matching forecasts.  The core PCE rose 2.8% from a year earlier, also matching forecasts.

title: Stocks Are Rallying Again as Fears of an Escalated Trade War Ease
summary: Fears of an escalated trade war ease after as President Donald Trump walks back tariffs after what he called a "total access" deal for Greenland.  The Dow is up 390 points, or 0.8%, putting it on pace for a weekly gain.  Trump has called off European tariffs on Greenland, citing a “framework of a future deal.”

title: EXCLUSIVE: Why The Silver Boom Could Get An Accidental Fed Boost
summary: Interest-rate cuts have always been gold's moment. This cycle, silver could quietly steal the spotlight. As U.S. debt piles up and the cost of servicing it rises, markets are increasingly pricing a more accommodative Federal Reserve —regardless of sticky inflation. That shift, according to Ed Egilinsky, Managing Director and Head of Sales, Distribution & Alternatives at Direxion, could create an unusually powerful macro setup for silver. title: Silver hits record high above $100/oz as gold closes in on $5,000 mark
summary:
STORY: Silver prices rose above $100 an ounce for the first time ever Friday, while gold hit another record as it marches toward $5000 an ounce.

Investors are piling into safe-haven assets amid geopolitical turmoil and on expectations for U.S. interest rate cuts.

One metals analyst told Reuters that silver is also benefiting from ongoing tariff concerns.

The metal has surged more than 200% in the past year, also driven by ongoing challenges in scaling up refining of the metal and a persistent supply shortage in the market.

Meanwhile, spot gold came within $12 of the $5,000 mark with gold futures a few dollars behind that.

One metals trader told Reuters that, "Gold's role as a haven and a diversifier in highly uncertain economic and political times is making it a necessity for strategic portfolios.”

Since the start of 2026, friction between the U.S. and NATO over Greenland, concerns about the Federal Reserve’s independence, and continued uncertainty over tariffs have driven a surge in demand for safe‑haven assets.

Central bank buying and a broader move away from the dollar have also underpinned gold's rise.
